model_summary: This initial observation model targets situationally predisposed panic waves in public with prominent chest tightness, fear of fainting, embarrassment, and pulse-checking. We retain five high-variance, momentary criteria (spike intensity, chest tightness, fainting belief, social embarrassment, and pulse-checking) and pair them with seven modifiable predictors spanning physiology (slow breathing use, sleep quality, caffeine), attention/cognition/behavior (grounding, reappraisal use, urge tolerance/response prevention), and contextual exposure (time in public). A 21-day EMA protocol with 5 prompts/day (since-last-prompt recall) plus a morning sleep diary balances statistical power and burden, optimizing within-person variance for gVAR while minimizing redundancy.
variable_selection_notes: Criteria were selected to preserve the core complaint dynamics while controlling collinearity: we included panic spike intensity (C01), chest tightness (C02), imminent-fainting belief (C03), embarrassment concern (C04), and pulse-checking (C05). We deprioritized escape planning (overlaps with safety behaviors), perceived unpredictability (risk of trait-like stationarity), and internal-attention shift (covered mechanistically by grounding predictor). Predictors were chosen to represent distinct, modifiable levers with strong literature and ranking support: physiological downshift (slow breathing), attentional grounding, cognitive reappraisal use, urge tolerance/response prevention, social exposure dose (nonlinear acute vs. longer-term effects), sleep quality (baseline arousal), and caffeine intake (arousal sensitizer). Trade-offs: we avoided wearables/neuromodulation/pharmacotherapy due to feasibility/scope and to keep a clean EMA-first model. Bias/uncertainty: social exposure effects are context-dependent and likely nonlinear; sleep has weekly cycles; caffeine effects vary by sensitivity. We use 1–9 Likert anchors to maximize variance and approximate continuity, and separate morning diary for sleep to avoid recall bias.

momentary_ema.notes: Randomize within each 2–3h block with ±10–20 min jitter. Snooze up to 10 min once per prompt. Encourage immediate responding to capture near-real-time states.

daily_diary.notes: Morning entry within 06:00–09:00 window to rate last night’s sleep. If missed, allow same-day catch-up by noon.

passive_streams.notes: Optional future add-ons: wearable HR/HRV and GPS crowd density; start EMA-only to reduce burden and simplify modeling.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: 5 EMA prompts/day over 21 days yields ~105 time points/variable (minus missingness). 1–9 Likert approximates continuous distributions for gVAR. Lags of 0–6h and 0–24h are compatible with the sampling interval. Daily diary (sleep) provides exogenous baseline input.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use anchored 1–9 scales; measure ‘since last prompt’ peak/intensity to avoid zeros. Include both symptom (C01–C02) and cognitive/behavioral (C03–C05) components to diversify within-person variance sources.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Higher evening nonresponse risk; use reminders and brief items (<10s/item). Consider adherence nudges on day 3 and 7. Define allowable ‘missed’ handling (carry-forward prohibited; listwise deletion or FIML later).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, what was the highest level of sudden anxiety or panic you experienced?
Since the last prompt, how tight did your chest feel at its worst?

criteria_variables.label: Belief of imminent fainting in public

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how strongly did you believe you might faint if symptoms continued (in public)?
measurement.notes: Captures catastrophic misinterpretation intensity during/after spikes.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how embarrassed or worried about others’ judgment did you feel when symptoms showed up?
Since the last prompt, how much did you check your pulse or other bodily signs?
measurement.notes: Captures both urge and enactment level.

predictor_variables.label: Slow breathing/arousal regulation use
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Longer-exhale/smooth breathing increases vagal tone and reduces sympathetic arousal, dampening panic intensity and somatic symptoms.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Box breathing, 4-6 breathing, paced breathing via app or watch prompt.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you use slow breathing (longer exhales) to calm your body?

expected_effects.rationale: Physiological downshift reduces peak panic intensity.
Reduces chest muscle tension and perceived constriction.
Lower arousal weakens catastrophic fainting appraisals.
Lower arousal decreases social-evaluative distress during spikes.
Less arousal reduces checking urges and enactment.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Breathing should be comfortable and not hyperventilatory; stop if lightheaded.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA item with high moment-to-moment variability.

predictor_variables.label: Attentional grounding (external focus)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Shifting attention outward counters interoceptive amplification and threat monitoring, reducing catastrophic interpretations and safety behaviors.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 5-senses grounding, counting external sounds, describing surroundings.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you deliberately focus outward on sights/sounds/people (grounding) when anxious?
measurement.notes: Captures skill use dose, not ability.

expected_effects.rationale: Reduces attention to internal threat cues driving panic surges.
Less interoceptive focus reduces perceived tightness intensity.
Disengaging from bodily cues reduces fainting expectancy.
Focus on environment dampens self-conscious processing.
Less internal scanning reduces checking behavior.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Use only when safe to attend to environment (e.g., not while driving beyond normal attention).

feasibility.notes: High day-to-day and intra-day variability expected.

predictor_variables.label: Cognitive reappraisal use (sensations are safe/temporary)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Reattributing sensations to benign arousal reduces catastrophic fainting beliefs and social-evaluative fear, dampening panic spirals.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: ‘This is uncomfortable, not dangerous’; probability reframe; window-of-tolerance framing.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you remind yourself that these sensations are intense but safe and temporary?
measurement.notes: Measures reappraisal use, not belief trait level.

expected_effects.rationale: Reduces perceived threat, lowering panic peaks.
Cognitive safety framing can reduce somatic vigilance and tension.
Directly targets fainting belief appraisals.
Safety reframe reduces embarrassment concern.
Lower perceived danger reduces checking behavior.

feasibility.notes: Brief, face-valid item with good variability.

predictor_variables.label: Urge tolerance/response prevention (stay vs. escape/check)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Inhibiting escape/checking increases safety learning and reduces reliance on safety behaviors, attenuating panic over time.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Urge surfing; stay-in-place for 1–3 minutes; compassionate self-talk while riding the wave.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, when you felt urges to escape or check your pulse, how much did you stay and ride it out?
measurement.notes: If no urge occurred, answer 1 (no opportunity) to keep scale consistent.

expected_effects.rationale: Acute containment reduces escalation and reinforces mastery.
Less escape checking reduces somatic amplification.
Successful tolerance updates beliefs about collapsing/fainting.
Mastery experiences reduce embarrassment concern.
Directly substitutes for checking/escape response.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Only practice in safe settings; do not persist if medically concerning symptoms occur.

feasibility.notes: Frequent opportunities create good variance; item wording clarifies if no urge occurred.

predictor_variables.label: Time in public/social places (exposure dose)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Greater exposure can transiently raise anxiety but promotes long-term inhibitory learning and reduced evaluative fear.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Graded in-vivo exposure (e.g., brief visits to crowded spaces, gradually increasing duration).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much time did you spend in public or social places?
measurement.notes: Captures context dose; effect likely nonlinear/lagged.

expected_effects.rationale: May acutely increase spikes but reduce over 24–48h via learning.
Somatic sensations may rise during exposure but reduce with practice.
Belief of fainting may initially increase, then decline with mastery.
Embarrassment may spike in-session and decrease with repeated exposure.
Checking may rise in-session but fall over time as safety learning accrues.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Use graded exposure; do not force high-intensity situations abruptly. Safety first.

feasibility.notes: Context is common in daily life; EMA recall is feasible.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep quality (last night)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Better sleep reduces baseline arousal and cognitive reactivity, lowering next-day panic susceptibility.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Consistent schedule, wind-down, light control, caffeine cut-off.

measurement.item_or_signal: This morning: How restorative was your sleep last night?

expected_effects.rationale: Improved sleep reduces next-day panic intensity risk.
Lower baseline tension decreases chest tightness reactivity.
Better sleep reduces catastrophic bias.
Improved affect regulation reduces embarrassment concern.
Lower arousal decreases checking urges.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Sleep hygiene advice only; no pharmacotherapy recommendations.

feasibility.notes: 1-item morning diary is low burden and robust.

predictor_variables.label: Caffeine intake (since last prompt)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Caffeine can increase sympathetic arousal and interoceptive sensations, elevating panic risk and checking.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Delay first caffeine, reduce dose, switch to lower-caffeine beverages.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much caffeine did you consume (coffee, tea, energy drinks)?
measurement.notes: No need to estimate mg; relative amount suffices for within-person modeling.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; include time-of-day controls in analysis

expected_effects.rationale: Stimulant effects elevate arousal and panic likelihood shortly after intake.
Increases palpitations and muscle tension perception.
Amplified sensations boost fainting expectations.
Higher arousal can heighten social-evaluative concern.
Greater arousal increases checking urges/behavior.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Avoid advising abrupt cessation if dependence suspected; suggest gradual adjustments.

edges.notes: Vagal activation lowers acute panic intensity.
Reduces muscle tension and perceived constriction.
Less attention to chest reduces perceived tightness.
Reappraisal lowers perceived threat driving spikes.
Less escape keeps arousal manageable; less somatic focus.
Likely acute increase then longer-term reduction.
Somatic sensations may spike in-session then drop.
Beliefs may initially worsen then improve with learning.
Evaluative fear may spike then reduce over time.
Checking may rise during exposure but fall with repetition.
Lower tension reduces chest tightness risk.
Caffeine acutely increases arousal and panic risk.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 with P02 (both reduce arousal/attention to threat)
P03 with C03 (conceptual proximity; mitigated by measuring P03 as use-of-reappraisal vs. belief strength)
P04 inversely related to C05 (behavioral substitution)
P05 with C04 during exposure windows (state coupling)
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C05 pulse-checking may be low outside public contexts (floor risk)
P04 urge tolerance may be inapplicable in blocks without urges (floor risk)
P05 exposure dose may be near-zero on stay-at-home days (floor risk)
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Keep prompts across social hours (late afternoon/evening included)
Anchor scales with clear extremes (1 and 9) to encourage full-range use
Encourage brief practice of skills (P01–P04) to generate variance
Sample weekends to capture different exposure and caffeine patterns
Add weekday/weekend indicators in modeling to handle cycles
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: C06 Immediate escape/exit planning (overlaps with safety-checking; kept P04 instead)
C07 Perceived unpredictability/lack of control (trait-like; risk of low within-person variance)
C08 Attention shift to internal sensations (covered by P02 grounding inverse)
Wearable HRV/HR (feasible later; excluded to simplify)
Pharmacotherapy and neuromodulation families (out of scope for self-guided EMA model)
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: 12 variables total (5 criteria + 7 predictors) with ~105 time points each (minus missingness) and clear lag structure (0–6h, 0–24h, multi-lag for exposure). Ordinal 1–9 items approximate continuous; within-person z-scoring recommended. Collinearity monitored; constructs are mechanism-distinct.

safety_notes: This is not a diagnosis. If chest pain is new, severe, or different from usual anxiety sensations, or if you have cardiac risk factors, seek urgent medical care. If panic or fainting concerns escalate, consult a qualified clinician. Use graded exposure safely; do not place yourself in unsafe situations. Reduce caffeine gradually if needed.
